county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Some early mining camp records in remote districts are scattered or lost.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Wasco County in 1862 during the eastern Oregon gold rush. Named for Edward D. Baker, U.S. Senator from Oregon killed at the Battle of Ball's Bluff (1861). Baker City was a supply hub for the gold mining districts of Auburn, Sumpter, and Cornucopia. The Oregon Trail passed through the county — the National Historic Oregon Trail Interpretive Center is located here.
county_metadata.research_tips: Baker County research centers on gold mining and the Oregon Trail. The Auburn mining district (1861) preceded the county itself, so check Wasco County for the earliest records. The Baker County Clerk has deed and mining claim records from 1862, and the Baker County Library holds an extensive local history collection with mining company records, family files, and photograph collections. The Sumpter Valley Railroad records document the logging and mining transportation network. Chinese miners were significant in Baker County — the Kam Wah Chung heritage site is nearby in Grant County, but Chinese Exclusion Act case files at NARA Seattle cover Baker County residents. FamilySearch has microfilmed Baker County marriage and probate records. The Oregon Trail rut sites and emigrant diaries at the Oregon Historical Society provide context for overland migration through this area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; index available in office, limited online access

source_profiles.source_name: Baker County Clerk — Land and Mining Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records and mining claims from county formation. Includes Auburn, Sumpter, and Cornucopia mining district records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no major fire losses.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: County marriage records from formation. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Baker County records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from county formation.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1870 and 1880 show gold mining population including Chinese miners.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving census years.

source_profiles.source_name: Chinese Exclusion Act Case Files — Baker County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Case files documenting Chinese miners, merchants, and laborers in Baker County gold mining districts. Includes photographs, affidavits, and testimony.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA Seattle holds Oregon district case files.

source_profiles.source_name: Baker City Herald and Mining Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baker City and mining district newspapers with legal notices, obituaries, mining news, and community events.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library and OHS.

source_profiles.repository: Baker County Circuit Court / Oregon State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate case files including wills, estate inventories, and guardianship records. Mining claim estates are particularly detailed.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older files transferred to Oregon State Archives.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Early provisional government records are sparse but survive.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Oregon's earliest counties, formed during the Provisional Government period in 1847 from Polk County. Named for Thomas Hart Benton, U.S. Senator from Missouri who championed westward expansion. Corvallis has been the county seat since 1851. Home to Oregon State University (founded 1868 as Corvallis College). Willamette Valley agricultural county with Donation Land Claims from the 1850s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Benton County is one of Oregon's original Provisional Government counties, so records reach back to the 1840s. Donation Land Claim files for Benton County settlers (1850–1855) are at NARA and indexed on the BLM GLO website — these are among the most genealogically rich records for early Oregon settlers, containing detailed biographical information including marriage dates and immigration dates. Oregon State University's Special Collections holds extensive Willamette Valley manuscript collections, agricultural records, and Corvallis community history materials. The Benton County Historical Society has family files and photograph collections. The county clerk has marriage records from 1850 and deed records from the territorial period. FamilySearch has microfilmed many Benton County records including probate and marriage files from the 1850s onward.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from territorial era; index available in office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era including Donation Land Claim transfers and Willamette Valley agricultural land sales.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no major fire losses.

source_profiles.source_name: Donation Land Claims — Benton County Settlers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Donation Land Claim files for Benton County settlers under the 1850 act. Contains settler biographical data, marriage information, and immigration history.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — indexed and searchable online at BLM GLO website.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from territorial period. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Benton County vital records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from territorial era.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1850–1950 (1890 destroyed). Oregon Territory census 1850 is the first enumeration. Includes agricultural schedules.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving census years. 1850 territorial census is available.

source_profiles.repository: Benton County Circuit Court / Oregon State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate case files, wills, estate inventories. Early cases reflect Donation Land Claim settlers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older records at Oregon State Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Corvallis Gazette-Times and Benton County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Corvallis newspapers including college town news, agricultural reports, legal notices, and obituaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Some early Provisional Government records from the 1840s are fragmentary but significant portions survive.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the four original districts created by Oregon's Provisional Government in 1843 — the oldest governmental divisions west of the Rocky Mountains in the United States. Oregon City served as the capital of the Provisional Government (1843–1848) and Oregon Territory (1848–1852). The Willamette Falls at Oregon City was the terminus of the Oregon Trail. Named for the Clackamas band of the Chinook people. John McLoughlin, the 'Father of Oregon,' settled here.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clackamas County holds some of the oldest American governmental records west of the Rockies, beginning with the Provisional Government era of 1843. Oregon City was the Oregon Trail's true terminus, and Provisional Government records — legislative journals, land claims, and early court records — are at the Oregon State Archives in Salem. The Clackamas County Clerk has deed records from the 1840s, and Donation Land Claims (1850–1855) for Clackamas settlers are rich biographical sources at BLM GLO Records. The Museum of the Oregon Territory in Oregon City houses emigrant artifacts and family files. McLoughlin House records and Hudson's Bay Company correspondence at the Oregon Historical Society document the pre-American era. FamilySearch has extensive Clackamas County microfilm including marriage, probate, and land records from the 1840s. The End of the Oregon Trail Interpretive Center has compiled emigrant arrival lists.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from Provisional Government era; online index available for recent records

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from the Provisional Government era — among the oldest American land records west of the Rockies.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no major fire losses. Provisional Government era records survive.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Donation Land Claim files for Oregon Trail settlers in Clackamas County. Exceptionally detailed biographical information for early Oregon settlers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — indexed and searchable online.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from Provisional Government era. Among the earliest civil marriage records in the Pacific Northwest.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — early records microfilmed by FamilySearch.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1850–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1850 territorial census is the first. Provisional Government census of 1845 also exists.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years.

source_profiles.source_name: Provisional Government and Territorial Court Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Provisional Government legislative records, early court proceedings, and territorial justice records from Oregon City — the seat of government.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — significant portions survive at Oregon State Archives. Some of the oldest government records in the Pacific Northwest.

source_profiles.source_name: Oregon City Enterprise and Territorial Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Oregon City newspapers from the territorial era. The Oregon Spectator (1846) was the first newspaper published west of the Rockies.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; OHS holds originals of early territorial papers.

county_metadata.record_losses: The Astoria fire of 1883 and 1922 fire destroyed some business and private records, but courthouse records were largely preserved.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Oregon's earliest counties, organized in 1844. Named for the Clatsop band of the Chinook people who inhabited the area. Astoria (founded 1811 by John Jacob Astor's Pacific Fur Company) is the oldest American settlement west of the Rockies. Fort Astoria was transferred to the British (1813) and returned after the War of 1812. Lewis and Clark wintered at Fort Clatsop (1805–1806). The Columbia River salmon canning industry centered here.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clatsop County holds records from the earliest American presence in the Pacific Northwest. The Astoria fur trade era (1811–1846) is documented through Hudson's Bay Company records at the Oregon Historical Society and British Columbia Archives. The Clatsop County Clerk has deed and marriage records from the 1840s. The Columbia River Maritime Museum in Astoria preserves fishing, shipping, and cannery records that document Scandinavian, Finnish, Chinese, and Japanese workers in the salmon canning industry. The Astoria Public Library has an extensive local history collection including ship passenger lists and fur trade documentation. Clatsop County Heritage Museum has family files and photographs. Check Fort Stevens military records (1863–1947) at NARA for military personnel stationed at the mouth of the Columbia. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Seaside and Cannon Beach areas were later developments.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from territorial era; index in office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Includes Astoria town lots and early fur trade era property transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — courthouse records survived Astoria fires of 1883 and 1922.

source_profiles.source_name: Columbia River Cannery and Maritime Worker Records
source_profiles.repository: Columbia River Maritime Museum / Clatsop County Heritage Museum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of salmon canning operations documenting Scandinavian, Finnish, Chinese, and Japanese workers. Employment records, cannery contracts, and fishing licenses.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — scattered across multiple repositories. Columbia River Maritime Museum has the best consolidated collection.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: County marriage records from territorial period. Includes marriages of fur trade era settlers and cannery workers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — early records survive and are microfilmed.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Military personnel records from Fort Stevens at the mouth of the Columbia River, active from Civil War through WWII. Only mainland US military installation attacked by Japan in WWII (1942).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds complete post records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1850–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1850 shows early Astoria settlement. Later census years capture fishing/canning industry workers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving census years.

source_profiles.source_name: Astoria Daily Astorian and Clatsop County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Daily Astorian and other Clatsop County papers covering maritime, fishing industry, and community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records are generally well preserved from county formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Washington County in 1854 during the Oregon Territory period. Named for the Columbia River, which forms the county's northern boundary. St. Helens has been the county seat since formation. Logging, fishing, and agriculture (dairy farming) were the primary industries. The county includes Sauvie Island, site of early Hudson's Bay Company farming operations. The town of Vernonia was a major logging center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Columbia County records are straightforward and well preserved from 1854 onward, with no courthouse fire losses. For pre-1854 records, check Washington County (the parent). The county clerk has deed and marriage records from formation. Logging company records from the Vernonia and Clatskanie areas are scattered — some at the Oregon Historical Society, others in private collections. Sauvie Island (originally 'Wapato Island') has Hudson's Bay Company connections; HBC records at the Oregon Historical Society and British Columbia Archives may mention early settlers. The Columbia County Historical Society in St. Helens has family files and photographs. FamilySearch has microfilmed some Columbia County records. The Columbia River fishing industry generated commercial fishing license records through the Oregon Fish Commission. Donation Land Claims for Columbia County are at BLM GLO.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; limited online index

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes timber land transactions and Columbia River waterfront properties.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no fire losses.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Donation Land Claim files for Columbia County settlers with biographical data.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — searchable online.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from 1854.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1860–1950 (1890 destroyed). Shows logging, fishing, and dairy farming communities.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving census years.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Helens Sentinel-Mist and Columbia County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Columbia County newspapers covering logging, fishing, and community news along the lower Columbia River.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. The county seat moved from Empire City to Coquille in 1896; records were transferred intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Umpqua and Jackson counties in 1853. Named for the Coos people, a Native American group of the southern Oregon coast. County seat was Empire City (1853–1896), then moved to Coquille. Coos Bay (originally Marshfield) was a major lumber shipping port and coal mining center. The Coquille Indian Tribe is headquartered in the county. Home to the Southern Oregon coast's timber and shipping industries.
county_metadata.research_tips: Coos County research must account for the county seat move from Empire City to Coquille in 1896 — records were transferred but researchers should verify location for older records. The Coos County Clerk in Coquille has deed and marriage records from 1853. The Coos History Museum in North Bend has excellent collections covering the lumber industry, coal mining at Libby and other sites, and the Coos Bay shipping port. The Coquille Indian Tribe has cultural and genealogical records for tribal members — the tribe was terminated in 1954 and restored in 1989, and BIA records at NARA cover the termination-era documentation. Scandinavian and Finnish immigrants were prominent in the logging and fishing industries. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Coos County records. Check the Southern Oregon Historical Society for records that overlap with Jackson County's parent relationship to Coos.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; in-office index

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation including timber land, coal mining claims, and Coos Bay waterfront properties.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — records survived county seat move in 1896.

source_profiles.source_name: Coquille Indian Tribe and Coos Bay BIA Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for Coos, Lower Umpqua, Siuslaw, and Coquille peoples. Includes census rolls, allotment records, and termination-era files (1954). Tribe restored in 1989.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA Seattle holds BIA records for western Oregon agencies.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Includes Scandinavian and Finnish immigrant families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from 1853.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1860–1950 (1890 destroyed). Shows coal mining, timber, and fishing communities.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving census years.

source_profiles.source_name: Coos Bay Times and Southern Oregon Coast Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Coos Bay and Coquille newspapers covering lumber shipping, coal mining, and coastal community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records are well preserved from county formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Wasco County in 1882. Named for General George Crook, who led military campaigns against Native Americans in central Oregon in the 1860s. Prineville has been the county seat since formation. Central Oregon ranching and lumber county. The Ochoco National Forest covers much of the county. Crook County was the parent county for both Jefferson (1914) and Deschutes (1916) counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Crook County originally encompassed much of central Oregon before Jefferson (1914) and Deschutes (1916) counties were carved from it, so pre-1914 records for those areas are in Crook County files. The county clerk in Prineville has deed records from 1882 including homestead entries and ranching land. The Bowman Museum (Crook County Historical Society) in Prineville has family files, photographs, and ranching industry records. Stock raising and cattle ranching dominated — brand registration records at the state level supplement local deed records. The Ochoco mining district had some gold and silver activity. FamilySearch has microfilmed Crook County records. Check Wasco County (parent) for pre-1882 records of the central Oregon area. Prineville was Oregon's first incorporated city east of the Cascades.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; in-office access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes ranching land, homestead entries, and Ochoco mining claims. Contains records for areas later split into Jefferson and Deschutes counties.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no major fire losses.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation covering central Oregon ranching families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from 1882.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1890–1950 (1890 destroyed). Pre-1882 enumeration under Wasco County. 1900 and 1910 cover areas later split to Jefferson and Deschutes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years (1890 destroyed nationally).

source_profiles.source_name: BLM Homestead and Land Patents — Crook County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for homestead entries in central Oregon ranching territory.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — searchable online.

source_profiles.source_name: Crook County Journal and Prineville Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Prineville newspapers covering ranching, lumber, and central Oregon community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Early records are sparse due to small population and remote location.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Coos County in 1855. Named for George Law Curry, the last Territorial Governor of Oregon. Gold Beach (named for gold found on the beach in the 1850s) has been the county seat. Oregon's most remote and least populated coastal county. The Rogue River Indian War of 1855–1856 displaced Native peoples from this area. Port Orford was an early settlement and military post.
county_metadata.research_tips: Curry County is one of Oregon's most remote counties, and early records are sparse — the population was under 1,000 well into the 1900s. The county clerk in Gold Beach has deed and marriage records from 1855, though early records are thin. Port Orford was the original settlement, and military records from the Rogue River War period (1855–1856) at NARA document both soldiers and Native Americans displaced from the area. Beach gold mining in the 1850s–1860s drew some miners. The Curry County Historical Society in Gold Beach has family files and local history materials. The Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest records at NARA include timber sale files that can identify logging families. FamilySearch has limited microfilm for Curry County. Brookings, the county's largest town today, was not established until 1908 as a lumber company town. Check Coos County (parent) for pre-1855 records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; very limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes beach gold mining claims and timber land transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from formation, though early records are sparse due to low population.

source_profiles.source_name: Rogue River War and Port Orford Military Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Military records from the Rogue River War period including Port Orford post records. Documents soldiers, volunteers, and Native American removal from the southern Oregon coast.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds the federal military records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Early records are sparse.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — early marriages are few due to small population.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1860–1950 (1890 destroyed). Very small population counts in early decades.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving census years.

source_profiles.source_name: Curry County Reporter and Gold Beach Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Gold Beach and Brookings area newspapers. Late start reflects remote nature of the county.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Limited digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records begin at county formation in 1916; earlier records are in Crook County.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Crook County in 1916. Named for the Deschutes River. Bend has been the county seat since formation. The county was created during the central Oregon railroad and lumber boom. Shevlin-Hixon and Brooks-Scanlon lumber companies dominated the economy from the 1910s through the 1950s. Now one of Oregon's fastest-growing counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Deschutes County was formed in 1916, so all earlier records for the Bend area are in Crook County (and before 1882, in Wasco County). The county clerk in Bend has deed and marriage records from 1916. The Deschutes County Historical Society and Museum in Bend holds lumber company records, particularly from Shevlin-Hixon and Brooks-Scanlon operations, which are invaluable for tracking logging families and mill workers. The Deschutes National Forest records at NARA include timber sale files and ranger station records. Homestead patent records for central Oregon are at BLM GLO — many settlers arrived during the 1900–1916 homestead boom. FamilySearch has microfilmed Deschutes County records from formation. The Bend Bulletin newspaper (founded 1903) provides continuous local coverage. Camp Abbot (WWII engineer training center near Sunriver) generated military personnel records at NARA.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; online index available for recent records

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes lumber company land transactions and homestead entries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no fire losses.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM Homestead and Timber Patents — Deschutes County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for central Oregon homestead entries during the settlement boom. Many settlers arrived before county formation.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — searchable online.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Pre-1916 marriages filed in Crook County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from 1916.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1920 (first after county formation). Pre-1916 area enumerated under Crook County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving census years.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Personnel and training records from Camp Abbot, a WWII Army engineer replacement training center near present-day Sunriver.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds federal military installation records.

source_profiles.source_name: Bend Bulletin and Deschutes County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Bend Bulletin (founded 1903 before county formation) and other central Oregon newspapers. Lumber industry news, homesteading, community events.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.
